engineering.translation: This verse carries the buddhi-yoga gateway's metaphysical injunction, and the engineering layer must remain scope-honest. Krishna tells Arjuna to be beyond the three modes — *sattva* (clarity), *rajas* (restlessness), *tamas* (inertia). That is an ontological claim, not an organisational one. The honest analog stays operational. A senior delivers steady work when the surrounding team is in *sattva* (clean execution), in *rajas* (demo-week chaos, racing a date), and in *tamas* (attrition, fogged morale) — without their own state being captured by the surrounding org-state. Most engineers function well only inside *sattva*. The verse points toward a settled centre that does not depend on which mode colours the team this week. Note carefully: this analog does NOT claim practitioners transcend ontology. It claims they can avoid being captured by team-state oscillation, which is a small operational reflection of a much larger metaphysical injunction. STRETCHED in scope.
engineering.concrete_scenario: Three Mondays observed at the same company. Monday A: the team sits inside *sattva*. The Postgres migration shipped clean, on-call has been quiet for two weeks, standup runs short, the architect updates the roadmap on Notion in eight minutes. The senior performs baseline work. Monday B: *rajas*. A founder pitch lands Wednesday, marketing requests three new screenshots, an executive injects a 'small ask' that becomes two days of work, standup drags forty minutes. The senior performs baseline work. Monday C: *tamas*. Two engineers gave notice last week, the launch has slipped a quarter, morale is heavy, standup is silent, the architect is on PTO. The senior performs baseline work. 'Baseline' here means the same elemental action — review the GitHub queue, run the failing test, check Datadog for an actual signal, propose the next concrete move. The verse is not asking the senior to feel nothing across these mornings. It names a condition in which decision-making does not collapse into the ambient mode. Juniors oscillate with their team. The figure described here remains untouched by the momentum-versus-decay duality, and remains unanxious about acquiring next quarter's wins or preserving last quarter's gains. They are self-possessed: a source of action that remains independent of which morning it happens to be.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to justify emotional dissociation from teammates — 'rising above' morale to the point of ignoring attrition, dismissing inertia signals, or treating restlessness-panic as someone else's problem. That is not transcendence; it is dissociation, itself a form of inertia. The check is whether action remains accurate across all three states. A senior who claims to be 'unaffected' yet whose review quality drops when the team enters *tamas* has transcended nothing. The diagnostic lives in artifacts produced — the pull request review, the design doc, the incident postmortem — never in the operator's self-report.
engineering.counter_example: When a team faces genuine crisis — not *rajas*-busyness but actual existential threat (regulatory filing, security breach, runway under thirty days) — the verse does not ask the operator to stay serene-but-passive. Action issuing from a settled state is still action; being beyond the three modes is not outcome-detachment. Whoever fails to adjust intensity when the situation truly differs has confused 'not captured by state' with 'not recognising state'.
engineering.implication: Notice your work across three Mondays. If quality oscillates with the team's mode — sharp in *sattva*, frantic in *rajas*, fogged in *tamas* — the steadiness described here is not yet established. The practice is not flattening emotion; it is keeping the substrate connected to a centre that does not move with the surrounding org-state.
engineering.quotable_line: Most engineers ship well only inside sattva; the senior this verse names performs the same baseline across all three Mondays.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.45 carries the metaphysically loaded nistrai-guṇya injunction. CRITICAL CARE per task spec: the engineering analog must NOT claim engineers can transcend ontology. It must stay at the operational layer (the senior who is not captured by team-state oscillation). The translation field opens with explicit scope-naming — 'this is the metaphysically loaded verse of the buddhi-yoga gateway' — and tags the engineering analog as STRETCHED in scope. Concrete scenario uses the three-Mondays framing: sattva (clean Postgres migration, calm standup), rajas (founder pitch + injected work), tamas (attrition + slipped launch). Named tools: Postgres, Notion, GitHub, Datadog. The senior produces the same baseline action across all three. Tags chosen from controlled vocabulary: sattva-clarity, rajas-restlessness, tamas-inertia, team-state. Per indian-philosopher persona protection: confidence=MEDIUM, stretched=true, with the verse explicitly acknowledging that 'the original verse is doing more than the analog can carry'. Falsifiability handles the dissociation failure mode (claimed unaffectedness without artifact evidence). Counter-example carves out the actual-crisis case (regulatory, security, runway).

v2 editorial pass: replaced technical Sanskrit terms in engineering register with English equivalents (nistrai-guṇya → 'beyond the three modes' / 'being beyond the three modes'; nirdvandva → 'untouched by the momentum-versus-decay duality'; niryoga-kṣema → 'unanxious about acquiring next quarter's wins or preserving last quarter's gains'; ātmavān → self-possessed). The three modes themselves (sattva, rajas, tamas) kept as italicised terms because they are load-bearing for the verse's diagnostic naming and the chapter glossary tags depend on them. Split compound sentences. Each Monday-scenario broken into discrete clauses with prose connectives. Doctrinal STRETCHED scope-tagging unchanged.
